does: This paper identifies and demonstrates a fundamental vulnerability in guardrail systems that screen prompts before they reach large language models. The authors introduce the Prompt Overflow Attack, which exploits the mismatch between guardrails' limited context windows (which use truncation or segmentation to process long inputs) and the much larger context windows of downstream LLMs. The attack fragments malicious instructions and intersperses them with benign filler content across an overlong prompt, ensuring that no individual segment inspected by the guardrail appears harmful while the complete context remains actionable to the LLM. Through systematic evaluation against state-of-the-art guardrail models including Meta Llama Prompt Guard, the authors demonstrate that this architectural mismatch enables adversarial prompts to bypass existing safety mechanisms.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks against LLMs and prompt injection defenses. While previous studies have examined semantic adversarial inputs and jailbreaking techniques that manipulate prompt content directly, this paper pivots to exploit the structural properties of safety infrastructure itself. It builds on understanding of guardrail deployment practices—where safety checkers screen inputs before inference—and connects to research on context window limitations and segmentation strategies in language models. The paper responds to growing awareness that safety mechanisms can create new attack surface when there are capability mismatches between defense and inference components, a concern previously underexplored in the guardrail literature.
enables: This work opens several important directions for improving LLM safety. It motivates the need for guardrails that inspect prompts using the same or larger context windows as the downstream model, rather than truncating or segmenting. It suggests that safety designers must account for the full prompt structure an LLM will process, not just individual segments. Future work could explore dynamic inspection strategies, adaptive segmentation that preserves semantic integrity, or guardrails that simulate downstream inference. The findings also highlight a broader design principle: safety mechanisms must match the capabilities and constraints of the systems they protect, pointing toward more holistic safety architectures that consider end-to-end prompt flow rather than isolated checkpoints.
